verses.commentary: Paul identifies himself as Christ's prisoner for the Gentiles' sake. His imprisonment serves God's purposes for Gentile inclusion. Suffering is vocational, not punitive.
Paul's stewardship of God's grace was for the Gentiles' benefit. Grace given to him was not for himself alone but to dispense to others. Ministry is grace-administration.
The mystery was made known by revelation, which Paul previously wrote about. Revelation, not human discovery, discloses God's hidden plan. Divine initiative unveils what was concealed.
Reading Paul's words enables understanding his insight into Christ's mystery. Written revelation preserves and communicates apostolic understanding. Scripture makes divine mystery accessible.

Previous generations did not know the mystery as it is now revealed by the Spirit. Progressive revelation means later clarity exceeds earlier understanding. The Spirit reveals to apostles and prophets.
The mystery is Gentiles' full equality with Israel: co-heirs, co-members, co-sharers in the promise. Three 'togethers' emphasize complete inclusion. The gospel erases ethnic privilege.
Paul became a servant by God's grace and power. Ministry effectiveness depends on divine enablement, not personal qualification. Grace transforms inadequacy into fruitful service.
Paul considers himself less than the least saint yet received grace to preach Christ's boundless riches to Gentiles. Self-assessment as least does not disqualify but highlights grace's magnitude.
Paul's mission is making plain the mystery's administration, hidden for ages in God. Stewardship involves disclosure. What God created and concealed, He now reveals.
Through the church, God's manifold wisdom is made known to spiritual powers in heavenly realms. The church is God's exhibit of wisdom to cosmic observers. Redemption displays divine genius.
This was God's eternal purpose accomplished in Christ. History unfolds according to pre-temporal plan. Christ executes what God eternally purposed.
In Christ, believers approach God with freedom and confidence through faith. Access to God is bold, not timid. Faith in Christ removes fear and grants intimacy.
Paul's sufferings for them are their glory, not cause for discouragement. Apostolic suffering advances the gospel and benefits believers. Persecution validates ministry.
Paul kneels before the Father, posture expressing reverence and earnestness. Prayer is the appropriate response to God's mercy and mystery. Intercession follows proclamation.
Every family in heaven and earth derives its name from the Father. Fatherhood originates with God; human fatherhood is derivative. God is the archetypal Father.
Paul prays for inner strengthening through the Spirit from God's glorious riches. Inner being needs divine power, which God supplies abundantly. Strength is spiritual, not physical.
Christ dwelling in hearts through faith produces rootedness in love. Christ's indwelling is by faith, resulting in love's foundation. Intimacy with Christ establishes love.
Paul prays believers comprehend Christ's love's dimensions with all saints. Love's vastness requires corporate exploration; no individual grasps it fully. Community enlarges understanding.
Knowing the love that surpasses knowledge is paradoxical. Experiential knowledge exceeds intellectual grasp. Being filled with God's fullness is the goal, achieved through knowing Christ's love.
God can do immeasurably more than we ask or imagine by His power in us. Divine capacity infinitely exceeds human expectation. God's work surpasses our greatest hopes.
Glory to God in the church and in Christ throughout all generations forever. The church glorifies God eternally. Praise is the church's perpetual purpose.
